participants.extracted: Thirty-four women with mixed urinary incontinence (MUI) were recruited from the Oslo area in Norway in August and September 2012. All were adults over 18 years of age, with a median age of 62.5 years (range 29–87 years). Participants were randomised to acupuncture (n=12), pelvic floor muscle training (n=10), or waiting list control (n=12) at baseline, although the number of women completing the study differed by group due to dropouts.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to one of three groups. In the acupuncture group, women received 12 sessions of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM)-based acupuncture over 12 weeks from an experienced acupuncturist. Treatment was individualized using a TCM diagnostic interview based on the four interrogations and eight-principles pattern identification, with semi-standardized point selection alternating between supine and prone positions. Points used included CV3, CV4, CV6, SP6, KI3, KI7, and, when indicated, PC6, LR3, and SP3; in prone position BL31–34, BL23, BL28, SP6, GV4, and GV20 were used, with BL14, BL18, and BL20 added for relevant symptoms. Sterile disposable needles were inserted to appropriate depth, de qi was obtained, and needles were retained for 30 minutes. In the physiotherapy group, women first had one individual consultation with a specialist female physiotherapist, including vaginal examination to confirm correct pelvic floor muscle contraction, and then attended 12 weekly group sessions of pelvic floor muscle training. The program included about 25 minutes of pelvic floor exercises plus 20 minutes of general exercise, and women were asked to perform pelvic floor muscle training at home for 10 minutes daily and keep an exercise diary. The waiting-list group received no active treatment during the 12-week study period and completed questionnaires at the end of follow-up; after the trial they were offered free choice of acupuncture or physiotherapy.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure urinary incontinence symptoms and treatment-related outcomes using the International Consultation on Incontinence Questionnaire-Urinary Incontinence Short Form (ICIQ-UI SF), a validated self-report scale assessing severity and bother of urinary leakage on a 0–21 scale. This was assessed at baseline and again at the endpoint after 12 weeks of treatment (post-intervention). The study also planned to measure participants’ expectations of treatment effect at baseline with two 1–11 linear rating questions: how much they thought acupuncture could help urinary incontinence, and how much they thought pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) could help urinary incontinence. Adverse events were planned to be recorded at the endpoint after 12 weeks using SurveyMonkey questions, including an open-ended question about any adverse events and a yes/no/do not know question about whether urinary leakage symptoms worsened at the beginning of treatment.
